notes: AKC #1 health concern for this breed. Every Frenchie owner should track breathing scores.

screening_freq: As needed if neurological signs appear
notes: French study found 45% of Frenchies with neurological signs had IVDD Type I. Avoid jumping and stairs.
Most cases are mild incidental findings on X-ray. Screw-tail is a visible indicator.

screening_freq: Once at 2 years for breeding stock
notes: Despite being small, Frenchies have high HD rates. OFA data: 30% dysplastic.
Medial luxation most common. Often coexists with hip dysplasia.

screening_test: Visual inspection of all skin folds
screening_freq: Daily by owner; vet check quarterly
notes: 11x more prevalent than general dog population. Daily wrinkle cleaning is essential.
Surgical replacement preferred over gland removal to preserve tear production.
Prominent eyes with poor blink coverage = constant risk. Any squinting = emergency vet visit.
Food allergy and environmental allergy both common. 72.4% of UK Frenchies had at least one health condition; skin problems most common group at 17.9%.
Often asymptomatic; only problematic if lashes contact cornea.

screening_freq: Once at 2 years for breeding stock
notes: One of the most screened conditions in Goldens. Responsible breeders test all breeding stock.

screening_freq: Once at 2 years for breeding stock
notes: Often concurrent with hip dysplasia. Forelimb lameness in a young Golden = suspect ED.

screening_test: Annual abdominal ultrasound (+ echocardiogram for cardiac form)
notes: Golden Retrievers have one of the highest HSA rates. 1 in 5 Goldens die of cancer; HSA is a leading cause for sudden death.
1 in 8 Goldens develop lymphoma. Early detection via routine lymph node checks improves outcomes.

screening_freq: DNA: once in lifetime; Eye exam: annually
notes: GR-PRA1 causes late-onset PRA. DNA testing of breeding stock can eliminate this from lines.
Hereditary juvenile cataracts appear in young dogs. Annual OFA eye exams detect early changes.
Preventable with gastropexy. Feed 2-3 smaller meals instead of 1 large. No exercise 1 hour after eating.

screening_test: OFA thyroid panel (T4, free T4, TSH, TgAA)
notes: Easily managed with daily levothyroxine. Screen annually after age 5.
Goldens are a poster breed for atopy. Ear infections are often the first sign.

screening_freq: Annually from age 1; every 6 months after age 5
age_of_onset: Early adult (50% have murmur by age 5)
notes: Essentially every Cavalier will develop MVD. Early detection + medication (pimobendan) can extend quality life by years. This is THE reason to use this app for Cavaliers.

screening_freq: Once for breeding stock; if symptomatic, as recommended
notes: Up to 70% of Cavaliers have CM/SM on MRI. Pain often missed by owners — 'air scratching' at neck is the hallmark sign.
Common in toy breeds. Grade 1-2 often managed conservatively; Grade 3-4 requires surgery.
Less common than in large breeds but still screened per CHIC protocol.
Hereditary cataracts appear in young Cavaliers. Annual eye exams detect early changes before vision is affected.
Often incidental; treat only if causing clinical signs.
Piebald gene linked. Parti-color Cavaliers (Blenheim, Tricolor) at higher risk than solid Ruby.

screening_freq: Annually from age 1 for breeding cats; every 1-2 years for pets
notes: Approximately 30% of Ragdolls carry the MYBPC3 mutation. Homozygous cats have more severe, earlier-onset disease. Annual echo is gold standard. The #1 cause of death in Ragdolls.

screening_test: Blood typing of queen and stud before breeding
notes: Ragdolls have high prevalence of Type B blood (~25%). Type B queen + Type A stud = NI risk. Always blood-type before breeding. Preventable with 100% effectiveness.
Stress is a major trigger. Ragdolls are sensitive cats; environmental enrichment and hydration are key prevention.

screening_freq: Annually from age 1-2 for breeding cats; every 1-2 years for pets
notes: Maine Coons have their own breed-specific MYBPC3 mutation (A31P, not R820W like Ragdolls). One of the most studied feline genetic diseases. Annual echo is essential — one normal echo does not guarantee future freedom from HCM.
Autosomal recessive — DNA test identifies carriers. Affected kittens show progressive weakness. Non-painful but reduces quality of life. Breeding carriers to carriers should be avoided.

screening_freq: Once for breeding cats at maturity
notes: Largest domestic cat breed → weight stress on joints. 18% of Maine Coons show radiographic HD. Maintain lean body weight.
Male Maine Coons at higher risk for urethral obstruction. Hydration, stress reduction, and regular urinalysis are key. A blocked cat is a dead cat in 24-48 hours without treatment.
Less common in Maine Coons than Persians but still present in the gene pool. Ultrasound is diagnostic.
